remarks.title: Unlocking Africa’s Economic Potential through Trade, Skills and Development Finance

speaker.name: Mathias Cormann
speaker.title: Secretary-General
speaker.organization: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)

summary.executive_summary: Mathias Cormann welcomed participants to the 2026 African Economic Conference and emphasized Africa’s potential to create millions of jobs, raise incomes, and expand opportunity. He organized his remarks around three priorities for unlocking this potential: trade and investment, education and skills, and private finance for development. On trade, he called for simpler rules, faster border procedures, greater data transparency, and continued digital-trade reforms. On skills, he highlighted the projected rise in the number of young Africans completing secondary or higher education and stressed that this demographic opportunity will generate productivity and better jobs only if education systems provide relevant skills. He encouraged wider African participation in the OECD’s Programme for International Student Assessment. On financing, he noted the sharp decline in bilateral official development assistance to Africa and argued that deeper, more integrated domestic capital markets can help cushion the impact. He proposed cross-listing and harmonized debt-market infrastructure to support regional market growth. Cormann also announced forthcoming consultations on an enhanced OECD partnership with Africa and the launch of an African Virtual Investment Platform with 15 pilot countries. He concluded by affirming the OECD’s commitment to remain a trusted partner in Africa’s transformation.
summary.purpose: To welcome participants, identify practical reforms for unlocking Africa’s economic potential, and outline the OECD’s current and planned engagement with African countries.

main_themes.description: Simpler trade rules, faster border procedures, stronger data transparency, and digital-trade reforms can reduce costs, expand exports, and attract higher-quality investment.
Africa’s rapidly expanding educated youth population represents a historic opportunity, provided education systems equip young people with skills that improve productivity and employment.
Deeper and more efficient domestic capital markets can help offset declining development assistance and expand financing for African development.
Cross-listing securities and harmonizing debt-market infrastructure could strengthen regional integration and generate economies of scale.
The OECD plans broader consultations and new investment-support initiatives with African institutions, regional organizations, and partner countries.

key_messages.message: Africa has enormous economic potential, but implementation, financing, and policy coordination remain central challenges.
Simpler trade rules, faster border procedures, and greater data transparency are needed to expand trade and attract quality investment.
Africa’s education expansion will translate into better jobs only if young people acquire the right skills.
Larger and more efficient African capital markets can help cushion the effects of shrinking development-assistance budgets.
Regional capital-market integration can be advanced through cross-listing and harmonized debt-market infrastructure.
The OECD intends to deepen its partnership with Africa and support countries in attracting more and higher-quality investment.

priorities_and_challenges: Implementing and financing proven reforms in a coordinated manner.
Reducing trade costs and improving border procedures.
Equipping a rapidly growing educated youth population with relevant skills.
Responding to the sharp decline in official development assistance.
Expanding capital-market activity beyond a small number of countries.
commitments_and_announcements: The OECD will begin consultations on an enhanced partnership with Africa.
Consultations will involve the African Union Commission, regional organizations, and partner countries.
The OECD will launch the African Virtual Investment Platform with 15 pilot countries.
The OECD intends to expand the investment platform to more countries across the continent.
The OECD pledged to remain a trusted partner in Africa’s economic transformation.
policy_implications: Governments should simplify trade regulations and accelerate customs and border procedures.
African countries should improve trade and investment data transparency.
Education policy should focus on learning quality and labor-market relevance, not only enrollment.
More countries could use international education assessments to guide reform.
Domestic capital markets should be broadened and made more efficient.
Regional exchanges and debt-market systems should become more interoperable.
calls_to_action: Build and accelerate trade and digital-reform momentum across Africa.
Equip young Africans with the skills required for productive employment.
Consider broader participation in the OECD’s Programme for International Student Assessment.
Develop larger and more efficient domestic capital markets.
Promote cross-listing and harmonize regional debt-market infrastructure.
Deepen cooperation among the OECD, African institutions, and partner countries.
partnerships_and_stakeholders: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development
summary.concluding_message: African countries are already making progress in trade, education, and access to capital, and the OECD will continue supporting efforts to accelerate this transformation.

powerful_quotes.context: Opening statement on the continent’s capacity to create jobs, raise incomes, and expand opportunity.

powerful_quotes.quote: This is a genuinely historic opportunity.
powerful_quotes.context: Referring to the projected rise in young Africans completing secondary or higher education.

powerful_quotes.quote: We invite others to consider joining.
powerful_quotes.context: Encouraging more African countries to participate in the OECD’s international student assessment programme.

powerful_quotes.quote: We will remain your trusted partner as we push this transformation further.
powerful_quotes.context: Concluding commitment to continued OECD support for Africa’s economic transformation.

highlights.text: The number of young Africans completing secondary or higher education is projected to rise from 103 million in 2020 to 240 million in 2040.
Bilateral official development assistance from OECD DAC members to Africa reportedly fell by 24% in real terms, from $36.4 billion in 2024 to $27.7 billion in 2025.
Egypt, Mauritius, Nigeria, and South Africa accounted for 61% of Africa’s outstanding corporate debt in 2024.
The OECD will consult the African Union Commission, regional organizations, and partner countries on an enhanced partnership with Africa.
An African Virtual Investment Platform will be launched with 15 pilot countries.
The OECD pledged to remain a trusted partner as Africa advances its economic transformation.

uncertain_items: The location and exact date are not stated in the transcript.
The transcript says the African Virtual Investment Platform will launch 'later this month' but does not provide an exact date.
The reference to 'last year' for South Africa’s digital licensing reform is relative to the event date.
The formal capitalization of the African Virtual Investment Platform was inferred from the spoken name.
